refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure and report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint or acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The system's functional role is to provide visibility into energy consumption and work output so that stakeholders can assess performance against targets. Achievement of the target is a property of the overall system operation and depends on design choices (DP/IM), operational practices, and external factors—not a requirement the system 'supports' in isolation. This refinement keeps the FR in the problem domain (what data/visibility is needed) and removes the solution-dependent notion of 'achieving' a target.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects, calculates, and reports energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved with defined accuracy and frequency over the evaluation period. Confirm that reported metrics are accessible to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Safety Manager, Maintenance/Quality) via defined interfaces.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure or infer total work output (tonnage moved) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall calculate energy efficiency metric (energy per ton) from measured inputs.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall record and report energy efficiency metrics to authorized stakeholders with defined temporal granularity.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period
DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors with local data aggregation; stream normalized metrics to central repository; compute and report energy_per_ton continuously with periodic validation
DP3: Establish energy accounting via fuel consumption logs and work tickets (manual or semi-automated); reconcile at period end; calculate energy_per_ton from aggregated records

DP4: Deploy dual measurement streams—energy via metered supply monitoring; work output via independent weighing/counting infrastructure—with asynchronous reconciliation and tolerance-based acceptance logic
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Deploy dual measurement streams—energy via metered supply monitoring; work output via independent weighing/counting infrastructure—with asynchronous reconciliation and tolerance-based acceptance logic

potential_sources: Energy and work-output measurement systems remain logically independent; failure in one does not invalidate the other
Asynchronous reconciliation decouples real-time operational constraints from acceptance-criteria validation
Tolerance-based logic isolates measurement uncertainty from functional correctness
Dual-stream architecture supports independent calibration, maintenance, and upgrade cycles
coupling_check.notes: DP4 avoids tight coupling present in DP2 (real-time dependency) and DP3 (manual reconciliation brittleness). DP1 is overly simplistic and offers no operational visibility. DP4 balances rigor (independent streams) with practicality (asynchronous reconciliation) and aligns with safety-critical measurement best practices.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source is metered or can be instrumented at a single point (e.g., fuel pump, electrical panel). Risk: Distributed or mobile energy sources may require multiple measurement points; mitigation: define energy boundary clearly in worksite plan.

Assumption: Work output (tonnage) can be measured independently of energy consumption (e.g., at weighbridge, not inferred from fuel burn). Risk: If tonnage is inferred from energy, the ratio becomes tautological. Mitigation: enforce independent measurement infrastructure.

Assumption: Measurement systems have sufficient accuracy and precision to satisfy E_target tolerance. Risk: Sensor drift, calibration drift, or environmental factors may degrade accuracy over evaluation period. Mitigation: establish pre- and post-evaluation calibration checkpoints.

Assumption: Reconciliation tolerance (acceptable discrepancy between energy and work records) is defined in acceptance criteria. Risk: Undefined tolerance leads to disputes. Mitigation: agree tolerance with stakeholders before evaluation begins.

Assumption: Data integrity and chain-of-custody are maintained (no tampering, loss, or corruption). Risk: Manual logs or unsecured data streams are vulnerable. Mitigation: implement audit trail and cryptographic integrity checks if high assurance required.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable independent measurement of energy consumption and work output (tonnage moved), with asynchronous reconciliation and tolerance-based acceptance, such that the computed ratio energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved satisfies ≤ E_target over the agreed evaluation period, with measurement systems calibrated before and after evaluation and reconciliation tolerance defined in the worksite acceptance criteria.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement reconciliation and energy-work accounting system that orchestrates dual measurement streams, manages asynchronous reconciliation logic, and enforces tolerance-based acceptance criteria across all deployed vehicles and teleoperation instances.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives energy consumption data from metered supply monitoring and work output data from independent weighing/counting infrastructure across all vehicles.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends reconciliation results, acceptance/rejection decisions, and tolerance violation alerts to Vehicle and Teleoperation for task adjustment or termination.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Escalates critical measurement discrepancies or reconciliation failures that exceed safety thresholds to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Manages asynchronous communication with distributed measurement endpoints and handles latency-tolerant reconciliation across network delays.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: NGHE Solution is the natural system-level orchestrator for multi-vehicle energy-work accounting. Dual measurement streams, reconciliation logic, and tolerance acceptance are inherently cross-vehicle concerns. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and tasking/safety escalation leak outward via defined interfaces. The asynchronous reconciliation engine and tolerance logic remain fully encapsulated.

Poor containment: Vehicle is a single-instance module. DP4 requires system-wide reconciliation across multiple vehicles and measurement sources. Placing dual measurement orchestration in Vehicle would require replication across all vehicle instances, creating severe coupling and inconsistent reconciliation state. Tolerance logic would diffuse across vehicle boundaries.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a control interface, not a measurement aggregation layer. Dual measurement streams and reconciliation logic are orthogonal to teleoperation concerns. Placing DP4 here would couple measurement accounting to operator control flow, creating tight coupling and poor separation of concerns. Diffusion across Vehicle and Safety Services would be high.

Poor containment: Safety Services is a reactive alert/enforcement module, not a measurement orchestration layer. While tolerance violations may trigger safety escalation, the core dual measurement and reconciliation logic is not a safety-specific concern. Placing DP4 here would conflate measurement accounting with safety policy, creating semantic confusion and high diffusion to Vehicle and Teleoperation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP4 (dual measurement streams with asynchronous reconciliation and tolerance-based acceptance) is fundamentally a system-level energy-work accounting concern that must operate across all vehicles and measurement endpoints. NGHE Solution is the only module with sufficient scope and authority to own the reconciliation orchestration, tolerance logic, and cross-vehicle state management without replication or diffusion. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are consumers of reconciliation results, not owners of the reconciliation mechanism itself.
containment_risks: Network latency in asynchronous reconciliation may create transient measurement inconsistency windows; NGHE Solution must define and enforce timeout/retry policies to prevent indefinite reconciliation hangs.

Tolerance thresholds are policy-driven and may require dynamic adjustment based on operational context (e.g., vehicle type, task criticality); NGHE Solution must expose tolerance configuration as an external parameter, not hardcode it.

Dual measurement stream synchronization depends on accurate timestamping and clock alignment across distributed endpoints; NGHE Solution must validate timestamp coherence and flag clock skew as a reconciliation failure mode.

Acceptance/rejection decisions may have downstream consequences (task abort, safety escalation); NGHE Solution must ensure idempotent decision logic and clear audit trails for all reconciliation outcomes.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 directly addresses the FR requirement to enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output. The dual measurement streams (energy via metered supply, work output via independent weighing/counting) with asynchronous reconciliation and tolerance-based acceptance logic provide a complete logical solution to the functional requirement. The DP is sufficiently detailed to guide implementation and is traceable to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target).

diffusion_risks: Tolerance threshold configuration is policy-driven and may require dynamic adjustment by operational context (vehicle type, task criticality, environmental conditions). Risk: If tolerance logic is hardcoded in NGHE Solution without external parameterization, policy changes will require code modification and redeployment, creating tight coupling to operational governance. Mitigation: Expose tolerance thresholds as externally configurable parameters with audit logging.

Timestamp coherence and clock synchronization across distributed measurement endpoints (metered supply monitors, weighing/counting infrastructure) is a prerequisite for valid reconciliation. Risk: If clock skew detection and correction logic is not explicitly owned by NGHE Solution, measurement endpoints may operate in divergent time domains, invalidating reconciliation windows. Mitigation: NGHE Solution must validate timestamp coherence on ingestion and flag clock skew as a reconciliation failure mode with explicit escalation.

Asynchronous reconciliation introduces transient measurement inconsistency windows during network latency or endpoint delays. Risk: If timeout/retry policies are not explicitly defined and enforced by NGHE Solution, reconciliation may hang indefinitely or produce stale decisions. Mitigation: Define explicit timeout windows, retry budgets, and fallback logic (e.g., accept partial reconciliation, escalate to Safety Services if timeout exceeded).

Acceptance/rejection decisions have downstream consequences (task abort, safety escalation, energy accounting). Risk: If decision idempotency and audit trail logic are not centralized in NGHE Solution, repeated reconciliation cycles or network retransmissions may produce inconsistent outcomes or duplicate accounting entries. Mitigation: Implement idempotent decision logic with unique reconciliation session IDs and immutable audit logs.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with minimal diffusion (0.08). The module is the natural system-level orchestrator for multi-vehicle energy-work accounting because: (1) Dual measurement stream orchestration is inherently cross-vehicle and cross-endpoint, requiring centralized coordination; (2) Asynchronous reconciliation logic must operate at system scope to maintain consistent state across all vehicles; (3) Tolerance-based acceptance is a policy-enforcing function that must be uniform across all measurement instances to ensure fair and auditable energy accounting. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are consumers of reconciliation results (via Tasking and SafetyAlert interfaces), not owners of the reconciliation mechanism. Placing DP4 in any of these modules would create severe replication, coupling, and inconsistency risks. The defined external interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking/SafetyAlert out, Network Link inout) are appropriate boundaries that preserve NGHE Solution's autonomy while enabling necessary information flow.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly measurable from NGHE Solution's reconciliation outputs. NGHE Solution must expose energy_per_ton as a reportable metric, computed as (total_energy_used / total_ton_moved) over the agreed evaluation period. Verification requires: (1) Audit trail of all dual measurement stream ingestions with timestamps; (2) Reconciliation decision log showing which measurements were accepted/rejected and why; (3) Final energy_per_ton calculation with tolerance application documented. The IM boundary provides a single point of measurement authority, enabling unambiguous verification against the acceptance criterion.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated entirely to NGHE Solution as the measurement and reporting orchestrator. The allocation is justified by: (1) NGHE Solution owns dual measurement stream ingestion (Telemetry interface); (2) NGHE Solution owns reconciliation logic and tolerance-based acceptance; (3) NGHE Solution owns reporting of energy_per_ton to external stakeholders (via Tasking interface for task-level reporting, and implicitly via data export for evaluation period reporting). Vehicle and Teleoperation are allocated no direct responsibility for energy-work accounting; they are consumers of reconciliation results. Safety Services is allocated responsibility for escalation handling (SafetyAlert interface) but not for measurement logic. This allocation is clean and avoids diffusion.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly define tolerance threshold parameterization in NGHE Solution's external interface contract. Specify: (a) tolerance value ranges (e.g., ±5% energy variance, ±2% work output variance); (b) tolerance adjustment triggers (e.g., by vehicle type, task criticality, environmental conditions); (c) configuration change audit logging and versioning. This prevents hardcoding and enables operational policy evolution without code changes.

Add timestamp coherence validation as a mandatory reconciliation precondition. Specify: (a) acceptable clock skew tolerance (e.g., ±100ms across all endpoints); (b) clock skew detection algorithm (e.g., statistical outlier detection on timestamp deltas); (c) escalation path when clock skew exceeds tolerance (flag as reconciliation failure, escalate to Safety Services, halt energy accounting until clock synchronization is restored).

Define explicit timeout and retry policies for asynchronous reconciliation. Specify: (a) reconciliation window timeout (e.g., 30 seconds from measurement ingestion to acceptance/rejection decision); (b) retry budget (e.g., up to 3 retries with exponential backoff); (c) fallback behavior when timeout is exceeded (e.g., accept partial reconciliation with reduced confidence, escalate to Safety Services, log as measurement anomaly). Document these policies in NGHE Solution's operational specification.

Implement idempotent reconciliation decision logic with immutable audit trails. Specify: (a) unique reconciliation session IDs (e.g., UUID per measurement pair); (b) decision immutability (once accepted/rejected, decision cannot be reversed without explicit audit override); (c) audit log schema capturing: timestamp, measurement pair ID, energy value, work output value, tolerance applied, decision (accept/reject), reason code, operator/system that triggered decision. This ensures energy accounting integrity and enables forensic analysis of measurement discrepancies.

Clarify the relationship between NGHE Solution's reconciliation results and downstream task execution. Specify: (a) when a measurement pair is rejected, what is the impact on the associated task (e.g., task abort, task retry, task flagged for manual review)?; (b) how does Tasking interface communicate rejection decisions back to Vehicle and Teleoperation?; (c) are there safety-critical thresholds (e.g., if >N consecutive rejections, escalate to Safety Services)? This prevents ambiguity in how reconciliation failures propagate to operational decisions.

Add explicit handling for partial measurement stream failures. Specify: (a) what happens if metered supply monitoring fails but weighing/counting infrastructure succeeds (or vice versa)?; (b) is reconciliation possible with only one stream, or must both streams be present?; (c) how long can NGHE Solution operate in degraded mode before escalating to Safety Services? This ensures robustness in the face of distributed endpoint failures.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the DP is logically sound and architecturally well-contained. However, the DP-IM gate has identified six critical diffusion risks that are safety-relevant and operationally consequential. Given the high safety criticality of energy-work accounting (which directly influences task abort, safety escalation, and energy compliance decisions), these risks must be explicitly addressed in the DP specification before decomposition proceeds. The recommended revisions are not minor clarifications—they are essential design decisions that affect reconciliation logic, failure modes, and audit integrity. Revising the current DP to incorporate these safeguards will: (1) eliminate ambiguity in tolerance parameterization, timestamp coherence, timeout/retry policies, idempotency, and partial failure handling; (2) reduce implementation risk by making these concerns explicit in the design domain rather than deferring them to implementation; (3) enable clearer allocation of sub-FRs at the next level by establishing firm boundaries around reconciliation orchestration, policy enforcement, and failure escalation. Proceeding to decomposition without these revisions would risk creating implementation modules that duplicate or scatter these concerns across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, violating the containment principle and creating the very diffusion risks the gate identified.

revise.why: DP4 (Dual measurement stream orchestration with asynchronous reconciliation and tolerance-based acceptance) is functionally correct but operationally incomplete. The DP must be expanded to explicitly specify: (1) Tolerance threshold parameterization mechanism (external configuration, audit logging, adjustment triggers by operational context); (2) Timestamp coherence validation as a mandatory precondition (clock skew tolerance, detection algorithm, escalation path); (3) Asynchronous reconciliation timeout and retry policies (window duration, retry budget, fallback behavior); (4) Idempotent decision logic with immutable audit trails (session IDs, decision immutability, audit log schema); (5) Downstream task execution impact (rejection consequences, Tasking interface communication, safety-critical escalation thresholds); (6) Partial measurement stream failure handling (single-stream reconciliation feasibility, degraded mode duration limits, escalation triggers). These additions transform DP4 from a high-level orchestration concept into a complete design specification that eliminates the identified diffusion risks and provides unambiguous guidance for implementation module boundaries.

assumptions: Tolerance thresholds are policy-driven and subject to operational adjustment by vehicle type, task criticality, and environmental conditions. Assumption: NGHE Solution must expose these as externally configurable parameters, not hardcoded constants.

Timestamp coherence across distributed measurement endpoints (metered supply monitors, weighing/counting infrastructure) is a prerequisite for valid reconciliation. Assumption: Clock skew detection and correction is a responsibility of NGHE Solution, not delegated to measurement endpoints.

Asynchronous reconciliation introduces transient measurement inconsistency windows. Assumption: Explicit timeout, retry, and fallback policies must be defined and enforced by NGHE Solution to prevent indefinite hangs or stale decisions.

Reconciliation decisions have downstream consequences (task abort, safety escalation, energy accounting). Assumption: Decision idempotency and immutable audit trails are essential to prevent duplicate accounting entries and ensure forensic traceability.

Measurement stream failures (metered supply or weighing/counting) may occur independently. Assumption: NGHE Solution must define whether reconciliation is possible with only one stream and how long degraded mode operation is acceptable before escalation.

Energy-work accounting is safety-critical because it influences task execution decisions and safety escalation. Assumption: All reconciliation logic, failure modes, and audit trails must be designed with safety integrity in mind, not as afterthoughts.
open_risks: Tolerance threshold configuration is policy-driven and may require dynamic adjustment. Risk: If tolerance logic is hardcoded in NGHE Solution without external parameterization, policy changes will require code modification and redeployment, creating tight coupling to operational governance. Mitigation: Expose tolerance thresholds as externally configurable parameters with audit logging and version control.

Timestamp coherence and clock synchronization across distributed measurement endpoints is a prerequisite for valid reconciliation. Risk: If clock skew detection and correction logic is not explicitly owned by NGHE Solution, measurement endpoints may operate in divergent time domains, invalidating reconciliation windows. Mitigation: NGHE Solution must validate timestamp coherence on ingestion and flag clock skew as a reconciliation failure mode with explicit escalation to Safety Services.

Asynchronous reconciliation introduces transient measurement inconsistency windows during network latency or endpoint delays. Risk: If timeout/retry policies are not explicitly defined and enforced by NGHE Solution, reconciliation may hang indefinitely or produce stale decisions. Mitigation: Define explicit timeout windows (e.g., 30 seconds), retry budgets (e.g., 3 retries with exponential backoff), and fallback logic (e.g., accept partial reconciliation, escalate to Safety Services if timeout exceeded).

Acceptance/rejection decisions have downstream consequences (task abort, safety escalation, energy accounting). Risk: If decision idempotency and audit trail logic are not centralized in NGHE Solution, repeated reconciliation cycles or network retransmissions may produce inconsistent outcomes or duplicate accounting entries. Mitigation: Implement idempotent decision logic with unique reconciliation session IDs (e.g., UUID per measurement pair) and immutable audit logs.

Partial measurement stream failures (metered supply or weighing/counting) may occur independently. Risk: If NGHE Solution does not explicitly define whether reconciliation is possible with only one stream and how long degraded mode operation is acceptable, operational ambiguity may lead to inconsistent energy accounting or safety escalation delays. Mitigation: Define explicit policies for single-stream reconciliation feasibility, degraded mode duration limits, and escalation triggers.

Reconciliation failures may propagate to task execution decisions. Risk: If the relationship between NGHE Solution's reconciliation results and downstream task execution (via Tasking interface) is not explicitly specified, rejected measurements may cause ambiguous task outcomes (abort, retry, manual review). Mitigation: Clarify task execution impact, Tasking interface communication protocol, and safety-critical escalation thresholds (e.g., if >N consecutive rejections, escalate to Safety Services).
evidence_needed: Specification of tolerance threshold parameterization mechanism: (a) tolerance value ranges (e.g., ±5% energy variance, ±2% work output variance); (b) tolerance adjustment triggers (e.g., by vehicle type, task criticality, environmental conditions); (c) configuration change audit logging and versioning schema.

Specification of timestamp coherence validation: (a) acceptable clock skew tolerance (e.g., ±100ms across all endpoints); (b) clock skew detection algorithm (e.g., statistical outlier detection on timestamp deltas); (c) escalation path when clock skew exceeds tolerance (flag as reconciliation failure, escalate to Safety Services, halt energy accounting until clock synchronization is restored).

Specification of asynchronous reconciliation timeout and retry policies: (a) reconciliation window timeout (e.g., 30 seconds from measurement ingestion to acceptance/rejection decision); (b) retry budget (e.g., up to 3 retries with exponential backoff); (c) fallback behavior when timeout is exceeded (e.g., accept partial reconciliation with reduced confidence, escalate to Safety Services, log as measurement anomaly).

Specification of idempotent reconciliation decision logic: (a) unique reconciliation session IDs (e.g., UUID per measurement pair); (b) decision immutability (once accepted/rejected, decision cannot be reversed without explicit audit override); (c) audit log schema capturing: timestamp, measurement pair ID, energy value, work output value, tolerance applied, decision (accept/reject), reason code, operator/system that triggered decision.

Specification of downstream task execution impact: (a) when a measurement pair is rejected, what is the impact on the associated task (e.g., task abort, task retry, task flagged for manual review)?; (b) how does Tasking interface communicate rejection decisions back to Vehicle and Teleoperation?; (c) are there safety-critical thresholds (e.g., if >N consecutive rejections, escalate to Safety Services)?

Specification of partial measurement stream failure handling: (a) what happens if metered supply monitoring fails but weighing/counting infrastructure succeeds (or vice versa)?; (b) is reconciliation possible with only one stream, or must both streams be present?; (c) how long can NGHE Solution operate in degraded mode before escalating to Safety Services?

Audit trail of all reconciliation decisions during pilot evaluation period, including: (a) measurement pair ingestion timestamps and values; (b) reconciliation decision logs (accepted/rejected, tolerance applied, reason codes); (c) any clock skew detections, timeout events, or partial stream failures; (d) downstream task execution impacts (task abort, retry, escalation); (e) final energy_per_ton calculation with tolerance application documented.
